target.name: Beacon AI
target.founder: Matt Cox, Avinash Nair, Andrew Musto

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $15M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $250M - $400.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Palantir
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $400B+

private.name: Liquid AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $2.35B

private.name: Field AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $2B

private.name: Twelve Labs
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $250M

data.narrative:
Beacon AI is a Series A-stage AI & Software company that has raised $15M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 9 peers (1 public, 8 private), including Palantir, Liquid AI, Field AI, and Twelve Labs at the same development stage.

Two triangulation methods were applied. The stage-progression approach uses the $15M total raised as a proxy base value and applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A progression, implying a $30M valuation. The peer-median method references four same-sector, same-stage comparables with valuations ranging from $250M to $400B, yielding a median of $2.2B. The wide range reflects material dispersion in the peer set, driven primarily by Palantir's $400B public valuation; excluding this outlier would substantially compress the range.

Triangulating both methods produces a low estimate of $26M, a median of $1.1B, and a high of $2.5B. The stage-progression result anchors the lower bound and reflects conservative reliance on capital raised. The peer-median dominates the upper bound but carries significant uncertainty due to peer-set heterogeneity and the outsized influence of late-stage, well-capitalized comparables. A defensible range narrows materially if Palantir is treated as a structural outlier rather than a direct comparable. Key uncertainties include product-market fit evidence, recurring revenue metrics, and the degree to which peer valuations reflect market timing rather than fundamental value.
